highlights.item: Payload is pointed at Supabase Postgres with migrations intentionally gated behind explicit user approval.
Bootstrap and seed flows exist for the first admin and repeatable content setup.

Payload MCP is now part of the repo process as an env-gated plugin path, with editor setup documented for Cursor and Codex, local configs normalized to `localhost:5465`, and a repo-owned stdio bridge for local MCP clients.
The customer login flow is now customer-only, while admin access stays on Payload admin and the internal ops dashboard lives on a separate `/ops` route.

Customer auth is now hybrid in repo: Supabase-backed password login, magic links, forgot/reset password screens, confirm/logout routes, and a bridge that resolves verified customer sessions back into Payload `users` while leaving staff auth on Payload.

Repo defaults for staff, preview, and demo identities now use the real `grimetime.app` domain family (`grimetime.app`, `demo.grimetime.app`) instead of old `.local` placeholders, which keeps Supabase customer auth and Resend sender setup aligned.
Customer-auth UX now explicitly blocks seeded `.local` addresses and points staff emails back to `/admin/login`, which avoids confusing Supabase invalid-email failures on local/dev-only addresses.

Account claiming and company invites now share one first-party token model stored on Payload `users`, with claim preview/request routes, claim completion on confirm, company-member invites from the account page, and a dedicated `/claim-account` route so the main login screen stays focused on password, magic-link, and create-account entry.

The customer-auth rollout is now complete for the current scope: quote, invoice, and appointment lifecycle emails can embed claim/invite CTAs so staff-created customers and company users have a direct activation path into the portal.

The portal sidebar and route boundaries now keep customers on account-centric pages (`/dashboard`, `/estimates`, `/invoices`, `/service-schedule`, `/account`) instead of exposing docs/admin-oriented navigation.

Admin impersonation is now first-party: a real-admin/effective-user auth context, admin-only impersonation routes, and a floating preview toolbar persist across frontend and portal pages so staff can preview customer scope without losing admin identity.

A vendored image-generation MCP server is now part of the repo workflow under `vendor/image-gen-mcp`, with a self-contained `uv` environment, root `.env` passthrough, Cursor/Codex config examples, and an env-gated `npm run dev` hook so image tooling can run alongside Next when needed.

Payload config now derives a deterministic fallback secret when `PAYLOAD_SECRET` is missing, which prevents Vercel prerender failures on public routes while still allowing an explicit secret to override it immediately.
The repo now pins Node to `20.x` in `package.json` so Vercel does not silently jump to a future major version during deploys.
Production builds now use `next build --webpack` because the current Vercel deployment path hit an ESM/CommonJS launcher mismatch with Turbopack under the repo's `type: module` scope.

next.item: Use the existing Payload MCP and image-generation MCP setup during normal content/data work instead of treating either server as experimental side tooling.
Decide whether public self-signup remains open or whether the long-term path becomes claim/invite-first for existing customers and company users.
Set `PAYLOAD_SECRET` explicitly in Vercel anyway; the fallback exists to keep deploys bootable, not to replace proper secret management.
Revisit Turbopack for production deploys after confirming the Vercel runtime no longer trips over the ESM/CommonJS page launcher path.
Decide whether admin sign-in should remain at `/admin/login` or later get a branded dedicated team-login route.
Backfill any real production customer accounts to the new portal profile fields if existing contacts need billing/service addresses immediately.
Use the new image MCP workflow in phase 03 to generate and curate hero, feature, and background assets, then decide which ones belong in Payload media versus `public/`.
